# Figure description: скв_167.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/drill_holes_data/скв_167.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite borehole/well log** (геологический разрез скважины or каротажная диаграмма) — a standard Soviet-era lithological-geophysical well log combining multiple geophysical curves with a lithological column and associated data tables. It appears to be a multi-track wireline log strip printed on a long vertical format sheet, characteristic of Soviet exploration drilling documentation from the 1970s–1980s.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log covers a single borehole (скважина), though the specific borehole number is **[illegible]** at this image resolution. The depth range appears to extend from approximately **0 m at the top to what appears to be 700–900+ m at the bottom**, based on the length of the strip and typical scale conventions. The lower portion of the sheet appears to show a separate, shorter interval or a continuation log, possibly representing a different borehole or a repeat section.

The log is associated with the northern Kazakhstan survey described in Report 36572, likely from map sheet area **M-42 or N-42**. No specific geographic locality name is clearly legible at this resolution.

---

## Key Content

The log is organized into multiple vertical tracks (columns) running from top to bottom, representing increasing depth. The following tracks are discernible:

### Left Side — Lithological Column and Core Description
- A **lithological column** (литологическая колонка) is visible on the left side, showing alternating rock types represented by standard Soviet geological symbols:
  - **Dotted/stippled patterns** — likely sandstones or sandy horizons
  - **Horizontal line patterns** — likely clays or mudstones/argillites
  - **Cross-hatch or brick patterns** — possibly carbonates or consolidated sediments
  - **Blank/white intervals** — possibly evaporites or massive units
- The lithological column shows significant variability throughout the section, with frequent alternation between lithologies, particularly in the upper and middle portions.
- Core recovery or sampling intervals may be indicated by tick marks or shading on an adjacent narrow column.

### Central Tracks — Geophysical Curves
The central portion of the log contains **two or three geophysical curve tracks** showing:
- **Spontaneous Potential (СП / SP) curve** — one of the smoother, more gently varying curves, used to identify permeable beds and correlate lithologies
- **Electrical Resistivity curves** — showing higher-amplitude, more jagged variations; likely includes both a short-spacing and long-spacing curve (КС — кажущееся сопротивление), possibly gradient and potential electrode configurations
- The curves show:
  - Several intervals of **high resistivity anomalies** (curves deflecting strongly to the right), particularly in the upper third and scattered through the middle section — potentially indicating consolidated, resistive, or mineralized horizons
  - Intervals of **low resistivity** (curves near baseline) corresponding to conductive units, likely clay-rich or water-saturated horizons
  - The SP curve shows clear deflections corresponding to permeable sandy beds vs. impermeable shales
- One track may represent a **gamma-ray log (ГК)** or **neutron log**, though this is uncertain at this resolution

### Right Side — Data Tables and Formation Tops
- To the right of the curves is a series of **tabular columns** containing:
  - Depth markers at regular intervals
  - Formation or stratigraphic unit designations (likely abbreviated formation names or stratigraphic codes — [illegible])
  - Numerical values in columns — possibly porosity estimates, resistivity values, or core analysis results
  - Several columns of small numbers arranged in a grid pattern, consistent with **petrophysical data tables** (e.g., Кп — porosity coefficient, Кн — water saturation, or similar parameters)
- A **legend or key** appears at the bottom right corner of the sheet, though its content is [illegible] at this resolution
- The far right columns contain what appear to be **age/stratigraphic designations** and possibly mineral or fluid content notations

### Lower Separate Section
- At the very bottom of the sheet there is a **shorter log strip** that appears to be a separate borehole or a zoomed interval, showing similar curve tracks over a shorter depth range. This may represent a **check well**, a **repeat section**, or a separate nearby borehole logged for correlation.

## Text and Labels

Due to the low resolution and faded nature of the scan, most text is partially or wholly illegible. The following elements are discernible:

**Header area (top of sheet):**
- Column headers in Russian — likely include: **БУЕНИЕ** or **БУРЕНИЕ** (Drilling), **ПРОМЫСЛОВАЯ ГЕОФИЗИКА** (Production/Field Geophysics) or similar
- Sub-headers likely reading: **Литологическая колонка** (Lithological column), **КС** (Apparent resistivity), **СП** (Spontaneous potential)
- Scale indicators for the geophysical curves — values [illegible]
- Depth scale notation — likely **М 1:500** or **М 1:200** (standard Soviet well log scales)

**Depth axis (leftmost column):**
- Depth values at regular intervals — appear to begin near **0** and increase downward; specific values [illegible] but spacing suggests **10 m or 20 m intervals** per graduation

**Right-side table headers:**

**Bottom right legend box:**
- Contains a small table or key — text [illegible], but structure suggests lithological symbol explanation or geophysical curve identification

**Annotations on the log itself:**
- Several horizontal lines crossing the full width of the log at specific depths — likely marking **formation boundaries** or **casing points**
- Small text annotations at some of these boundary lines — [illegible]

## Research Value

This composite well log is the primary subsurface dataset for characterizing the **lithological sequence, stratigraphic intervals, and petrophysical properties** of the drilled section in the northern Kazakhstan survey area.
